reasoning: Multiple leading climate organizations (NOAA, Copernicus, and Berkeley Earth) track global temperatures throughout the year. Based on data spanning January through September 2025, it is overwhelmingly evident that 2025 will not be the hottest year on record. Although 2025 began very warmly (January was a record), consecutive recent months including August and September 2025 ranked as merely the 3rd warmest of their respective months globally. Early in the year it started trailing 2024, and by August, NOAA's Global Annual Temperature Outlook estimated a less than 1% chance for 2025 to surpass 2024 as the warmest year. With La Niña conditions emerging toward the very end of 2025, which historically cools global surface temperatures, the final months of the year mathematically cannot make up the deficit to 2024. The probability is constrained at the lowest possible valid boundary (5%) per system rules.
